If no strong hand be nigh to prop the tottering wall.
ta:
இடுக்கண்கால் கொன்றிட வீழும் அடுத்தூன்றும்
  நல்லாள் இலாத குடி.

commentary: Agriculture, though laborious, is the most excellent (form of labour); for people, though they go about (in search of various employments), have at last to resort to the farmer.
en:
Howe'er they roam, the world must follow still the plougher's team;
Though toilsome, culture of the ground as noblest toil esteem.
ta:
சுழன்றும்ஏர்ப் பின்னது உலகம் அதனால்
  உழந்தும் உழவே தலை.

commentary: Agriculturists are (as it were) the linch-pin of the world for they support all other workers who cannot till the soil.
en:
The ploughers are the linch-pin of the world; they bear
Them up who other works perform, too weak its toils to share.
ta:
உழுவார் உலகத்தார்க்கு ஆணிஅஃ தாற்றாது
  எழுவாரை எல்லாம் பொறுத்து.

commentary: They alone live who live by agriculture; all others lead a cringing, dependent life.
en:
Who ploughing eat their food, they truly live:
The rest to others bend subservient, eating what they give.
ta:
உழுதுண்டு வாழ்வாரே வாழ்வார்மற் றெல்லாம்
  தொழுதுண்டு பின்செல் பவர்.

commentary: Patriotic farmers desire to bring all other states under the control of their own king.
en:
O'er many a land they 'll see their monarch reign,
Whose fields are shaded by the waving grain.
ta:
பலகுடை நீழலும் தங்குடைக்கீழ்க் காண்பர்
  அலகுடை நீழ லவர்.

commentary: Those whose nature is to live by manual labour will never beg but give something to those who beg.
en:
They nothing ask from others, but to askers give,
Who raise with their own hands the food on which they live.
ta:
இரவார் இரப்பார்க்கொன்று ஈவர் கரவாது
  கைசெய்தூண் மாலை யவர்.

commentary: If the farmer's hands are slackened, even the ascetic state will fail.
en:
For those who 've left what all men love no place is found,
When they with folded hands remain who till the ground.
ta:
உழவினார் கைம்மடங்கின் இல்லை விழைவதூஉம்
  விட்டேம்என் பார்க்கும் நிலை.

commentary: If the land is dried so as to reduce one ounce of earth to a quarter, it will grow plentifully even without a handful of manure.
en:
Reduce your soil to that dry state, When ounce is quarter-ounce's weight;
Without one handful of manure, Abundant crops you thus secure.
ta:
தொடிப்புழுதி கஃசா உணக்கின் பிடித்தெருவும்
  வேண்டாது சாலப் படும்.

commentary: Manuring is better than ploughing; after weeding, watching is better than watering (it).
en:
To cast manure is better than to plough;
Weed well; to guard is more than watering now
ta:
ஏரினும் நன்றால் எருவிடுதல் கட்டபின்
  நீரினும் நன்றதன் காப்பு.

commentary: If the owner does not (personally) attend to his cultivation, his land will behave like an angry wife and yield him no pleasure.
en:
When master from the field aloof hath stood;
Then land will sulk, like wife in angry mood.
ta:
செல்லான் கிழவன் இருப்பின் நிலம்புலந்து
  இல்லாளின் ஊடி விடும்.

commentary: The maiden, Earth, will laugh at the sight of those who plead poverty and lead an idle life.
en:
The earth, that kindly dame, will laugh to see,
Men seated idle pleading poverty.
ta:
இலமென்று அசைஇ இருப்பாரைக் காணின்
  நிலமென்னும் நல்லாள் நகும்.

commentary: There is nothing that afflicts (one) like poverty.
en:
You ask what sharper pain than poverty is known;
Nothing pains more than poverty, save poverty alone.
ta:
இன்மையின் இன்னாதது யாதெனின் இன்மையின்
  இன்மையே இன்னா தது.

commentary: When cruel poverty comes on, it deprives one of both the present and future (bliss).
en:
Malefactor matchless! poverty destroys
This world's and the next world's joys.
ta:
இன்மை எனவொரு பாவி மறுமையும்
  இம்மையும் இன்றி வரும்.

commentary: Hankering poverty destroys at once the greatness of (one's) ancient descent and (the dignity of one's) speech.
en:
Importunate desire, which poverty men name,
Destroys both old descent and goodly fame.
ta:
தொல்வரவும் தோலும் கெடுக்கும் தொகையாக
  நல்குரவு என்னும் நசை.

commentary: Even in those of high birth, poverty will produce the fault of uttering mean words.
en:
From penury will spring, 'mid even those of noble race,
Oblivion that gives birth to words that bring disgrace.
ta:
இற்பிறந்தார் கண்ணேயும் இன்மை இளிவந்த
  சொற்பிறக்கும் சோர்வு தரும்.

commentary: The misery of poverty brings in its train many (more) miseries.
en:
From poverty, that grievous woe,
Attendant sorrows plenteous grow.
ta:
நல்குரவு என்னும் இடும்பையுள் பல்குரைத்
  துன்பங்கள் சென்று படும்.

commentary: The words of the poor are profitless, though they may be sound in thought and clear in expression.
en:
Though deepest sense, well understood, the poor man's words convey,
Their sense from memory of mankind will fade away.
ta:
நற்பொருள் நன்குணர்ந்து சொல்லினும் நல்கூர்ந்தார்
  சொற்பொருள் சோர்வு படும்.

commentary: He that is reduced to absolute poverty will be regarded as a stranger even by his own mother.
en:
From indigence devoid of virtue's grace,
The mother e'en that bare, estranged, will turn her face.
ta:
அறஞ்சாரா நல்குரவு ஈன்றதா யானும்
  பிறன்போல நோக்கப் படும்.

commentary: Is the poverty that almost killed me yesterday, to meet me today too ?
en:
And will it come today as yesterday,
The grief of want that eats my soul away?
ta:
இன்றும் வருவது கொல்லோ நெருநலும்
  கொன்றது போலும் நிரப்பு.

commentary: One may sleep in the midst of fire; but by no means in the midst of poverty.
en:
Amid the flames sleep may men's eyelids close,
In poverty the eye knows no repose.
ta:
நெருப்பினுள் துஞ்சலும் ஆகும் நிரப்பினுள்
  யாதொன்றும் கண்பாடு அரிது.

commentary: The destitute poor, who do not renounce their bodies, only consume their neighbour's salt and water.
en:
Unless the destitute will utterly themselves deny,
They cause their neighbour's salt and vinegar to die.
ta:
துப்புர வில்லார் துவரத் துறவாமை
  உப்பிற்கும் காடிக்கும் கூற்று.

commentary: If you meet with those that may be begged of, you may beg; (but) if they withhold (their gift) it is their blame and not yours.
en:
When those you find from whom 'tis meet to ask,- for aid apply;
Theirs is the sin, not yours, if they the gift deny.
ta:
இரக்க இரத்தக்கார்க் காணின் கரப்பின்
  அவர்பழி தம்பழி அன்று.

commentary: Even begging may be pleasant, if what is begged for is obtained without grief (to him that begs).
en:
Even to ask an alms may pleasure give,
If what you ask without annoyance you receive.
ta:
இன்பம் ஒருவற்கு இரத்தல் இரந்தவை
  துன்பம் உறாஅ வரின்.

commentary: There is even a beauty in standing before and begging of those who are liberal in their gifts and understand their duty (to beggars).
en:
The men who nought deny, but know what's due, before their face
To stand as suppliants affords especial grace.
ta:
கரப்பிலா நெஞ்சின் கடனறிவார் முன்நின்று
  இரப்புமோ ரேஎர் உடைத்து.

commentary: To beg of such as never think of withholding (their charity) even in their dreams, is in fact the same as giving (it oneself);
en:
Like giving alms, may even asking pleasant seem,
From men who of denial never even dream.
ta:
இரத்தலும் ஈதலே போலும் கரத்தல்
  கனவிலும் தேற்றாதார் மாட்டு.

commentary: As there are in the world those that give without refusing, there are (also) those that prefer to beg by simply standing before them.
en:
Because on earth the men exist, who never say them nay,
Men bear to stand before their eyes for help to pray.
ta:
கரப்பிலார் வையகத்து உண்மையால் கண்ணின்று
  இரப்பவர் மேற்கொள் வது.

commentary: All the evil of begging will be removed at the sight of those who are far from the evil of refusing.
en:
It those you find from evil of 'denial' free,
At once all plague of poverty will flee.
ta:
கரப்பிடும்பை யில்லாரைக் காணின் நிரப்பிடும்பை
  எல்லாம் ஒருங்கு கெடும்.

commentary: Beggars rejoice exceedingly when they behold those who bestow (their alms) with kindness and courtesy.
en:
If men are found who give and no harsh words of scorn employ,
The minds of askers, through and through, will thrill with joy.
ta:
இகழ்ந்தெள்ளாது ஈவாரைக் காணின் மகிழ்ந்துள்ளம்
  உள்ளுள் உவப்பது உடைத்து.

commentary: If there were no beggars, (the actions done in) the cool wide world would only resemble the movement of a puppet.
en:
If askers cease, the mighty earth, where cooling fountains flow,
Will be a stage where wooden puppets come and go.
ta:
இரப்பாரை இல்லாயின் ஈர்ங்கண்மா ஞாலம்
  மரப்பாவை சென்றுவந் தற்று.

commentary: What (praise) would there be to givers (of alms) if there were no beggars to ask for and reveive (them).
en:
What glory will there be to men of generous soul,
When none are found to love the askers' role?
ta:
ஈவார்கண் என்னுண்டாம் தோற்றம் இரந்துகோள்
  மேவார் இலாஅக் கடை.

commentary: He who begs ought not to be angry (at a refusal); for even the misery of (his own) poverty should be a sufficient reason (for so doing).
en:
Askers refused from wrath must stand aloof;
The plague of poverty itself is ample proof.
ta:
இரப்பான் வெகுளாமை வேண்டும் நிரப்பிடும்பை
  தானேயும் சாலும் கரி.

commentary: Not to beg (at all) even from those excellent persons who cheerfully give without refusing, will do immense good.
en:
Ten million-fold 'tis greater gain, asking no alms to live,
Even from those, like eyes in worth, who nought concealing gladly give.
ta:
கரவாது உவந்தீயும் கண்ணன்னார் கண்ணும்
  இரவாமை கோடி உறும்.

commentary: If the Creator of the world has decreed even begging as a means of livelihood, may he too go abegging and perish.
en:
If he that shaped the world desires that men should begging go,
Through life's long course, let him a wanderer be and perish so.
ta:
இரந்தும் உயிர்வாழ்தல் வேண்டின் பரந்து
  கெடுக உலகியற்றி யான்.

commentary: There is no greater folly than the boldness with which one seeks to remedy the evils of poverty by begging (rather than by working).
en:
Nothing is harder than the hardness that will say,
'The plague of penury by asking alms we'll drive away.'
ta:
இன்மை இடும்பை இரந்துதீர் வாமென்னும்
  வன்மையின் வன்பாட்ட தில்.

commentary: Even the whole world cannot sufficiently praise the dignity that would not beg even in the midst of destitution.
en:
Who ne'er consent to beg in utmost need, their worth
Has excellence of greatness that transcends the earth.
ta:
இடமெல்லாம் கொள்ளாத் தகைத்தே இடமில்லாக்
  காலும் இரவொல்லாச் சால்பு.

commentary: Even thin gruel is ambrosia to him who has obtained it by labour.
en:
Nothing is sweeter than to taste the toil-won cheer,
Though mess of pottage as tasteless as the water clear.
ta:
தெண்ணீர் அடுபுற்கை ஆயினும் தாள்தந்தது
  உண்ணலின் ஊங்கினிய தில்.

commentary: There is nothing more disgraceful to one's tongue than to use it in begging water even for a cow.
en:
E'en if a draught of water for a cow you ask,
Nought's so distasteful to the tongue as beggar's task.
ta:
ஆவிற்கு நீரென்று இரப்பினும் நாவிற்கு
  இரவின் இளிவந்த தில்.

commentary: I beseech all beggars and say, "If you need to beg, never beg of those who give unwillingly."
en:
One thing I beg of beggars all, 'If beg ye may,
Of those who hide their wealth, beg not, I pray.'
ta:
இரப்பன் இரப்பாரை எல்லாம் இரப்பின்
  கரப்பார் இரவன்மின் என்று.

commentary: The unsafe raft of begging will split when it strikes on the rock of refusal.
en:
The fragile bark of beggary
Wrecked on denial's rock will lie.
ta:
இரவென்னும் ஏமாப்பில் தோணி கரவென்னும்
  பார்தாக்கப் பக்கு விடும்.

commentary: To think of (the evil of) begging is enough to melt one's heart; but to think of refusal is enough to break it.
en:
The heart will melt away at thought of beggary,
With thought of stern repulse 'twill perish utterly.
ta:
இரவுள்ள உள்ளம் உருகும் கரவுள்ள
  உள்ளதூஉம் இன்றிக் கெடும்.

commentary: Saying 'No' to a beggar takes away his life. (but as that very word will kill the refuser) where then would the latter's life hide itself ?
en:
E'en as he asks, the shamefaced asker dies;
Where shall his spirit hide who help denies?
ta:
கரப்பவர்க்கு யாங்கொளிக்கும் கொல்லோ இரப்பவர்
  சொல்லாடப் போஒம் உயிர்.

commentary: The base resemble men perfectly (as regards form); and we have not seen such (exact) resemblance (among any other species).
en:
The base resemble men in outward form, I ween;
But counterpart exact to them I've never seen.
ta:
மக்களே போல்வர் கயவர் அவரன்ன
  ஒப்பாரி யாங்கண்ட தில்.

commentary: The low enjoy more felicity than those who know what is good; for the former are not troubled with anxiety (as to the good).
en:
Than those of grateful heart the base must luckier be,
Their minds from every anxious thought are free!
ta:
நன்றறி வாரிற் கயவர் திருவுடையர்
  நெஞ்சத்து அவலம் இலர்.

commentary: The base resemble the Gods; for the base act as they like.
en:
The base are as the Gods; they too
Do ever what they list to do!
ta:
தேவர் அனையர் கயவர் அவருந்தாம்
  மேவன செய்தொழுக லான்.

commentary: The base feels proud when he sees persons whose acts meaner than his own.
en:
When base men those behold of conduct vile,
They straight surpass them, and exulting smile.
ta:
அகப்பட்டி ஆவாரைக் காணின் அவர஧ன்
  மிகப்பட்டுச் செம்மாக்கும் கீழ்.

commentary: (The principle of) behaviour in the mean is chiefly fear; if not, hope of gain, to some extent.
en:
Fear is the base man's virtue; if that fail,
Intense desire some little may avail.
ta:
அச்சமே கீழ்களது ஆசாரம் எச்சம்
  அவாவுண்டேல் உண்டாம் சிறிது.

commentary: The base are like a drum that is beaten, for they unburden to others the secrets they have heard.
en:
The base are like the beaten drum; for, when they hear
The sound the secret out in every neighbour's ear.
ta:
அறைபறை அன்னர் கயவர்தாம் கேட்ட
  மறைபிறர்க்கு உய்த்துரைக்க லான்.

commentary: The mean will not (even) shake off (what sticks to) their hands (soon after a meal) to any but those who would break their jaws with their clenched fists.
en:
From off their moistened hands no clinging grain they shake,
Unless to those with clenched fist their jaws who break.
ta:
ஈர்ங்கை விதிரார் கயவர் கொடிறுடைக்கும்
  கூன்கையர் அல்லா தவர்க்கு.

commentary: The great bestow (their alms) as soon as they are informed; (but) the mean, like the sugar-cane, only when they are tortured to death.
en:
The good to those will profit yield fair words who use;
The base, like sugar-cane, will profit those who bruise.
ta:
சொல்லப் பயன்படுவர் சான்றோர் கரும்புபோல்
  கொல்லப் பயன்படும் கீழ்.

commentary: The base will bring an evil (accusation) against others, as soon as he sees them (enjoying) good food and clothing.
en:
If neighbours clothed and fed he see, the base
Is mighty man some hidden fault to trace?
ta:
உடுப்பதூஉம் உண்பதூஉம் காணின் பிறர்மேல்
  வடுக்காண வற்றாகும் கீழ்.

commentary: The base will hasten to sell themselves as soon as a calamity has befallen them. For what else are they fitted ?
en:
For what is base man fit, if griefs assail?
Himself to offer, there and then, for sale!
ta:
எற்றிற் குரியர் கயவரொன்று உற்றக்கால்
  விற்றற்கு உரியர் விரைந்து.

commentary: Is this jewelled female a celestial, a choice peahen, or a human being ? My mind is perplexed.
en:
Goddess? or peafowl rare? She whose ears rich jewels wear,
Is she a maid of human kind? All wildered is my mind!
ta:
அணங்குகொல் ஆய்மயில் கொல்லோ கனங்குழை
  மாதர்கொல் மாலும்என் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: This female beauty returning my looks is like a celestial maiden coming with an army to contend against me.
en:
She of the beaming eyes, To my rash look her glance replies,
As if the matchless goddess' hand Led forth an armed band.
ta:
நோக்கினாள் நோக்கெதிர் நோக்குதல் தாக்கணங்கு
  தானைக்கொண் டன்ன துடைத்து.

commentary: I never knew before what is called Yama; I see it now; it is the eyes that carry on a great fight with (the help of) female qualities.
en:
Death's form I formerly Knew not; but now 'tis plain to me;
He comes in lovely maiden's guise, With soul-subduing eyes.
ta:
பண்டறியேன் கூற்றென் பதனை இனியறிந்தேன்
  பெண்டகையால் பேரமர்க் கட்டு.

commentary: These eyes that seem to kill those who look at them are as it were in hostilities with this feminine simplicity.
en:
In sweet simplicity, A woman's gracious form hath she;
But yet those eyes, that drink my life, Are with the form at strife!
ta:
கண்டார் உயிருண்ணும் தோற்றத்தால் பெண்டகைப்
  பேதைக்கு அமர்த்தன கண்.

commentary: Is it Yama, (a pair of) eyes or a hind ?- Are not all these three in the looks of this maid ?
en:
The light that on me gleams, Is it death's dart? or eye's bright beams?
Or fawn's shy glance? All three appear In form of maiden here.
ta:
கூற்றமோ கண்ணோ பிணையோ மடவரல்
  நோக்கமிம் மூன்றும் உடைத்து.

commentary: Her eyes will cause (me) no trembling sorrow, if they are properly hidden by her cruel arched eye-brows.
en:
If cruel eye-brow's bow, Unbent, would veil those glances now;
The shafts that wound this trembling heart Her eyes no more would dart.
ta:
கொடும்புருவம் கோடா மறைப்பின் நடுங்கஞர்
  செய்யல மன்இவள் கண்.

commentary: The cloth that covers the firm bosom of this maiden is (like) that which covers the eyes of a rutting elephant.
en:
As veil o'er angry eyes Of raging elephant that lies,
The silken cincture's folds invest This maiden's panting breast.
ta:
கடாஅக் களிற்றின்மேற் கட்படாம் மாதர்
  படாஅ முலைமேல் துகில்.

commentary: On her bright brow alone is destroyed even that power of mine that used to terrify the most fearless foes in the battlefield.
en:
Ah! woe is me! my might, That awed my foemen in the fight,
By lustre of that beaming brow Borne down, lies broken now!
ta:
ஒண்ணுதற் கோஒ உடைந்ததே ஞாட்பினுள்
  நண்ணாரும் உட்குமென் பீடு.

commentary: Of what use are other jewels to her who is adorned with modesty, and the meek looks of a hind ?
en:
Like tender fawn's her eye; Clothed on is she with modesty;
What added beauty can be lent; By alien ornament?
ta:
பிணையேர் மடநோக்கும் நாணும் உடையாட்கு
  அணியெவனோ ஏதில தந்து.

commentary: Unlike boiled honey which yields delight only when it is drunk, love gives pleasure even when looked at.
en:
The palm-tree's fragrant wine, To those who taste yields joys divine;
But love hath rare felicity For those that only see!
ta:
உண்டார்கண் அல்லது அடுநறாக் காமம்போல்
  கண்டார் மகிழ்செய்தல் இன்று.

commentary: There are two looks in the dyed eyes of this (fair one); one causes pain, and the other is the cure thereof.
en:
A double witchery have glances of her liquid eye;
One glance is glance that brings me pain; the other heals again.
ta:
இருநோக்கு இவளுண்கண் உள்ளது ஒருநோக்கு
  நோய்நோக்கொன் றந்நோய் மருந்து.

commentary: A single stolen glance of her eyes is more than half the pleasure (of sexual embrace).
en:
The furtive glance, that gleams one instant bright,
Is more than half of love's supreme delight.
ta:
கண்களவு கொள்ளும் சிறுநோக்கம் காமத்தில்
  செம்பாகம் அன்று பெரிது.

commentary: She has looked (at men) and stooped (her head); and that (sign) waters as it were (the corn of) our love.
en:
She looked, and looking drooped her head:
On springing shoot of love 'its water shed!
ta:
நோக்கினாள் நோக்கி இறைஞ்சினாள் அஃதவள்
  யாப்பினுள் அட்டிய நீர்.

commentary: When I look, she looks down; when I do not, she looks and smiles gently.
en:
I look on her: her eyes are on the ground the while:
I look away: she looks on me with timid smile.
ta:
யான்நோக்கும் காலை நிலன்நோக்கும் நோக்காக்கால்
  தான்நோக்கி மெல்ல நகும்.

commentary: She not only avoids a direct look at me, but looks as it were with a half-closed eye and smiles.
en:
She seemed to see me not; but yet the maid
Her love, by smiling side-long glance, betrayed.
ta:
குறிக்கொண்டு நோக்காமை அல்லால் ஒருகண்
  சிறக்கணித்தாள் போல நகும்

commentary: Though they may speak harshly as if they were strangers, the words of the friendly are soon understood.
en:
Though with their lips affection they disown,
Yet, when they hate us not, 'tis quickly known.
ta:
உறாஅ தவர்போல் சொலினும் செறாஅர்சொல்
  ஒல்லை உணரப் படும்.

commentary: Little words that are harsh and looks that are hateful are (but) the expressions of lovers who wish to act like strangers.
en:
The slighting words that anger feign, while eyes their love reveal.
Are signs of those that love, but would their love conceal.
ta:
செறாஅச் சிறுசொல்லும் செற்றார்போல் நோக்கும்
  உறாஅர்போன்று உற்றார் குறிப்பு.

commentary: When I look, the pitying maid looks in return and smiles gently; and that is a comforting sign for me.
en:
I gaze, the tender maid relents the while;
And, oh the matchless grace of that soft smile!
ta:
அசையியற்கு உண்டாண்டோர் ஏஎர்யான் நோக்கப்
  பசையினள் பைய நகும்.

commentary: Both the lovers are capable of looking at each other in an ordinary way, as if they were perfect strangers.
en:
The look indifferent, that would its love disguise,
Is only read aright by lovers' eyes.
ta:
ஏதிலார் போலப் பொதுநோக்கு நோக்குதல்
  காதலார் கண்ணே உள.

commentary: The words of the mouths are of no use whatever, when there is perfect agreement between the eyes (of lovers).
en:
When eye to answering eye reveals the tale of love,
All words that lips can say must useless prove.
ta:
கண்ணொடு கண்இணை நோக்கொக்கின் வாய்ச்சொற்கள்
  என்ன பயனும் இல.

commentary: The (simultaneous) enjoyment of the five senses of sight, hearing, taste, smell and touch can only be found with bright braceleted (women).
en:
All joys that senses five- sight, hearing, taste, smell, touch- can give,
In this resplendent armlets-bearing damsel live!
ta:
கண்டுகேட்டு உண்டுயிர்த்து உற்றறியும் ஐம்புலனும்
  ஒண்தொடி கண்ணே உள.

commentary: The remedy for a disease is always something different (from it); but for the disease caused by this jewelled maid, she is herself the cure.
en:
Disease and medicine antagonists we surely see;
This maid, to pain she gives, herself is remedy.
ta:
பிணிக்கு மருந்து பிறமன் அணியிழை
  தன்நோய்க்குத் தானே மருந்து.

commentary: Can the lotus-eyed Vishnu's heaven be indeed as sweet to those who delight to sleep in the delicate arms of their beloved ?
en:
Than rest in her soft arms to whom the soul is giv'n,
Is any sweeter joy in his, the Lotus-eyed-one's heaven?
ta:
தாம்வீழ்வார் மென்றோள் துயிலின் இனிதுகொல்
  தாமரைக் கண்ணான் உலகு.

commentary: From whence has she got this fire that burns when I withdraw and cools when I approach ?
en:
Withdraw, it burns; approach, it soothes the pain;
Whence did the maid this wondrous fire obtain?
ta:
நீங்கின் தெறூஉம் குறுகுங்கால் தண்ணென்னும்
  தீயாண்டுப் பெற்றாள் இவள்.

commentary: The shoulders of her whose locks are adorned with flowers delight me as if they were the very sweets I have desired (to get).
en:
In her embrace, whose locks with flowery wreaths are bound,
Each varied form of joy the soul can wish is found.
ta:
வேட் ட பொழுதின் அவையவை
  போலுமே தோட் டார் கதுப்பினாள்
  தோள்.

commentary: The shoulders of this fair one are made of ambrosia, for they revive me with pleasure every time I embrace them.
en:
Ambrosia are the simple maiden's arms; when I attain
Their touch, my withered life puts forth its buds again!
ta:
உறுதோறு உயிர்தளிர்ப்பத் தீண்டலால் பேதைக்கு
  அமிழ்தின் இயன்றன தோள்.

commentary: The embraces of a gold-complexioned beautiful female are as pleasant as to dwell in one's own house and live by one's own (earnings) after distributing (a portion of it in charity).
en:
As when one eats from household store, with kindly grace
Sharing his meal: such is this golden maid's embrace.
ta:
தம்மில் இருந்து தமதுபாத்து உண்டற்றால்
  அம்மா அரிவை முயக்கு.

commentary: To ardent lovers sweet is the embrace that cannot be penetrated even by a breath of breeze.
en:
Sweet is the strict embrace of those whom fond affection binds,
Where no dissevering breath of discord entrance finds.
ta:
வீழும் இருவர்க்கு இனிதே வளியிடை
  போழப் படாஅ முயக்கு.

commentary: Love quarrel, reconciliation and intercourse - these are the advantages reaped by those who marry for lust.
en:
The jealous variance, the healing of the strife, reunion gained:
These are the fruits from wedded love obtained.
ta:
ஊடல் உணர்தல் புணர்தல் இவைகாமம்
  கூடியார் பெற்ற பயன்.

commentary: As (one's) ignorance is discovered the more one learns, so does repeated intercourse with a well-adorned female (only create a desire for more).
en:
The more men learn, the more their lack of learning they detect;
'Tis so when I approach the maid with gleaming jewels decked.
ta:
அறிதோறு அறியாமை கண்டற்றால் காமம்
  செறிதோறும் சேயிழை மாட்டு.

commentary: May you flourish, O Anicham! you have a delicate nature. But my beloved is more delicate than you.
en:
O flower of the sensitive plant! than thee
More tender's the maiden beloved by me.
ta:
நன்னீரை வாழி அனிச்சமே நின்னினும்
  மென்னீரள் யாம்வீழ் பவள்.

commentary: O my soul, fancying that flowers which are seen by many can resemble her eyes, you become confused at the sight of them.
en:
You deemed, as you saw the flowers, her eyes were as flowers, my soul,
That many may see; it was surely some folly that over you stole!
ta:
மலர்காணின் மையாத்தி நெஞ்சே இவள்கண்
  பலர்காணும் பூவொக்கும் என்று.

commentary: The complexion of this bamboo-shouldered one is that of a shoot; her teeth, are pearls; her breath, fragrance; and her dyed eyes, lances.
en:
As tender shoot her frame; teeth, pearls; around her odours blend;
Darts are the eyes of her whose shoulders like the bambu bend.
ta:
முறிமேனி முத்தம் முறுவல் வெறிநாற்றம்
  வேலுண்கண் வேய்த்தோ ளவட்கு.

commentary: If the blue lotus could see, it would stoop and look at the ground saying, "I can never resemble the eyes of this excellent jewelled one."
en:
The lotus, seeing her, with head demiss, the ground would eye,
And say, 'With eyes of her, rich gems who wears, we cannot vie.'
ta:
காணின் குவளை கவிழ்ந்து நிலன்நோக்கும்
  மாணிழை கண்ணொவ்வேம் என்று.

commentary: No merry drums will be beaten for the (tender) waist of her who has adorned herself with the anicham without having removed its stem.
en:
The flowers of the sensitive plant as a girdle around her she placed;
The stems she forgot to nip off; they 'll weigh down the delicate waist.
ta:
அனிச்சப்பூக் கால்களையாள் பெய்தாள் நுகப்பிற்கு
  நல்ல படாஅ பறை.

commentary: The stars have become confused in their places not being able to distinguish between the moon and the maid's countenance.
en:
The stars perplexed are rushing wildly from their spheres;
For like another moon this maiden's face appears.
ta:
மதியும் மடந்தை முகனும் அறியா
  பதியின் கலங்கிய மீன்.

commentary: Could there be spots in the face of this maid like those in the bright full moon ?
en:
In moon, that waxing waning shines, as sports appear,
Are any spots discerned in face of maiden here?
ta:
அறுவாய் நிறைந்த அவிர்மதிக்குப் போல
  மறுவுண்டோ மாதர் முகத்து.

commentary: If you can indeed shine like the face of women, flourish, O moon, for then would you be worth loving ?
en:
Farewell, O moon! If that thine orb could shine
Bright as her face, thou shouldst be love of mine.
ta:
மாதர் முகம்போல் ஒளிவிட வல்லையேல்
  காதலை வாழி மத஧.

commentary: O moon, if you wish to resemble the face of her whose eyes are like (these) flowers, do not appear so as to be seen by all.
en:
If as her face, whose eyes are flowers, thou wouldst have charms for me,
Shine for my eyes alone, O moon, shine not for all to see!
ta:
மலரன்ன கண்ணாள் முகமொத்தி யாயின்
  பலர்காணத் தோன்றல் மதி.

commentary: The anicham and the feathers of the swan are to the feet of females, like the fruit of the (thorny) Nerunji.
en:
The flower of the sensitive plant, and the down on the swan's white breast,
As the thorn are harsh, by the delicate feet of this maiden pressed.
ta:
அனிச்சமும் அன்னத்தின் தூவியும் மாதர்
  அடிக்கு நெருஞ்சிப் பழம்.

commentary: The water which oozes from the white teeth of this soft speeched damsel is like a mixture of milk and honey.
en:
The dew on her white teeth, whose voice is soft and low,
Is as when milk and honey mingled flow.
ta:
பாலொடு தேன்கலந் தற்றே பணிமொழி
  வாலெயிறு ஊறிய நீர்.

commentary: The love between me and this damsel is like the union of body and soul.
en:
Between this maid and me the friendship kind
Is as the bonds that soul and body bind.
ta:
உடம்பொடு உயிரிடை என்னமற் றன்ன
  மடந்தையொடு எம்மிடை நட்பு.

commentary: O you image in the pupil (of my eye)! depart; there is no room for (my) fair-browed beloved.
en:
For her with beauteous brow, the maid I love, there place is none;
To give her image room, O pupil of mine eye, begone!
ta:
கருமணியிற் பாவாய்நீ போதாயாம் வீழும்
  திருநுதற்கு இல்லை இடம்.

commentary: My fair-jewelled one resembles the living soul (when she is in union with me), the dying soul when she leaves me.
en:
Life is she to my very soul when she draws nigh;
Dissevered from the maid with jewels rare, I die!
ta:
வாழ்தல் உயிர்க்கன்னள் ஆயிழை சாதல்
  அதற்கன்னள் நீங்கும் இடத்து.

commentary: If I had forgotten her who has bright battling eyes, I would have remembered (thee); but I never forget her. (Thus says he to her maid).
en:
I might recall, if I could once forget; but from my heart
Her charms fade not, whose eyes gleam like the warrior's dart.
ta:
உள்ளுவன் மன்யான் மறப்பின் மறப்பறியேன்
  ஒள்ளமர்க் கண்ணாள் குணம்.

commentary: My lover would not depart from mine eyes; even if I wink, he would not suffer (from pain); he is so ethereal.
en:
My loved one's subtle form departs not from my eyes;
I wink them not, lest I should pain him where he lies.
ta:
கண்ணுள்ளின் போகார் இமைப்பின் பருகுவரா
  நுண்ணியர்எம் காத லவர்.

commentary: As my lover abides in my eyes, I will not even paint them, for he would (then) have to conceal himself.
en:
My love doth ever in my eyes reside;
I stain them not, fearing his form to hide.
ta:
கண்ணுள்ளார் காத லவராகக் கண்ணும்
  எழுதேம் கரப்பாக்கு அறிந்து.

commentary: As my lover is in my heart, I am afraid of eating (anything) hot, for I know it would pain him.
en:
Within my heart my lover dwells; from food I turn
That smacks of heat, lest he should feel it burn.
ta:
நெஞ்சத்தார் காத லவராக வெய்துண்டல்
  அஞ்சுதும் வேபாக் கறிந்து.

commentary: I will not wink, knowing that if I did, my lover would hide himself; and for this reason, this town says, he is unloving.
en:
I fear his form to hide, nor close my eyes:
'Her love estranged is gone!' the village cries.
ta:
இமைப்பின் கரப்பாக்கு அறிவல் அனைத்திற்கே
  ஏதிலர் என்னும்இவ் வூர்.

commentary: My lover dwells in my heart with perpetual delight; but the town says he is unloving and (therefore) dwells afar.
en:
Rejoicing in my very soul he ever lies;
'Her love estranged is gone far off!' the village cries.
ta:
உவந்துறைவர் உள்ளத்துள் என்றும் இகந்துறைவர்
  ஏதிலர் என்னும்இவ் வூர்.

commentary: To those who after enjoyment of sexual pleasure suffer (for want of more), there is no help so efficient as the palmyra horse.
en:
To those who 've proved love's joy, and now afflicted mourn,
Except the helpful 'horse of palm', no other strength remains.
ta:
காமம் உழந்து வருந்தினார்க்கு ஏமம்
  மடலல்லது இல்லை வலி.

commentary: Having got rid of shame, the suffering body and soul save themselves on the palmyra horse.
en:
My body and my soul, that can no more endure,
Will lay reserve aside, and mount the 'horse of palm'.
ta:
நோனா உடம்பும் உயிரும் மடலேறும்
  நாணினை நீக்கி நிறுத்து.

commentary: Modesty and manliness were once my own; now, my own is the palmyra horse that is ridden by the lustful.
en:
I once retained reserve and seemly manliness;
To-day I nought possess but lovers' 'horse of palm'.
ta:
நாணொடு நல்லாண்மை பண்டுடையேன் இன்றுடையேன்
  காமுற்றார் ஏறும் மடல்.

commentary: The raft of modesty and manliness, is, alas, carried-off by the strong current of lust.
en:
Love's rushing tide will sweep away the raft
Of seemly manliness and shame combined.
ta:
காமக் கடும்புனல் உய்க்கும் நாணொடு
  நல்லாண்மை என்னும் புணை.

commentary: She with the small garland-like bracelets has given me the palmyra horse and the sorrow that is endured at night.
en:
The maid that slender armlets wears, like flowers entwined,
Has brought me 'horse of palm,' and pangs of eventide!
ta:
தொடலைக் குறுந்தொடி தந்தாள் மடலொடு
  மாலை உழக்கும் துயர்.

commentary: Mine eyes will not close in sleep on your mistress's account; even at midnight will I think of mounting the palmyra horse.
en:
Of climbing 'horse of palm' in midnight hour, I think;
My eyes know no repose for that same simple maid.
ta:
மடலூர்தல் யாமத்தும் உள்ளுவேன் மன்ற
  படல்ஒல்லா பேதைக்கென் கண்.

commentary: There is nothing so noble as the womanly nature that would not ride the palmyra horse, though plunged a sea of lust.
en:
There's nought of greater worth than woman's long-enduring soul,
Who, vexed by love like ocean waves, climbs not the 'horse of palm'.
ta:
கடலன்ன காமம் உழந்தும் மடலேறாப்
  பெண்ணின் பெருந்தக்க தில்.

commentary: Even the Lust (of women) transgresses its secrecy and appears in public, forgetting that they are too chaste and liberal (to be overcome by it).
en:
In virtue hard to move, yet very tender, too, are we;
Love deems not so, would rend the veil, and court publicity!
ta:
நிறையரியர் மன்அளியர் என்னாது காமம்
  மறையிறந்து மன்று படும்.

commentary: My lust, feeling that it is not known by all, reels confused in the streets (of this town).
en:
'There's no one knows my heart,' so says my love,
And thus, in public ways, perturbed will rove.
ta:
அறிகிலார் எல்லாரும் என்றேஎன் காமம்
  மறுகின் மறுகும் மருண்டு.

commentary: Even strangers laugh (at us) so as to be seen by us, for they have not suffered.
en:
Before my eyes the foolish make a mock of me,
Because they ne'er endured the pangs I now must drie.
ta:
யாம்கண்ணின் காண நகுப அறிவில்லார்
  யாம்பட்ட தாம்படா ஆறு.

commentary: My precious life is saved by the raise of rumour, and this, to my good luck no others are aware of.
en:
By this same rumour's rise, my precious life stands fast;
Good fortune grant the many know this not!
ta:
அலரெழ ஆருயிர் ந஧ற்கும் அதனைப்
  பலரறியார் பாக்கியத் தால்.

commentary: Not knowing the value of her whose eyes are like flowers this town has got up a rumour about me.
en:
The village hath to us this rumour giv'n, that makes her mine;
Unweeting all the rareness of the maid with flower-like eyne.
ta:
மலரன்ன கண்ணாள் அருமை அறியாது
  அலரெமக்கு ஈந்ததிவ் வூர்.

commentary: Will I not get a rumour that is known to the (whole) town ? For what I have not got is as if I had got it (already).
en:
The rumour spread within the town, is it not gain to me?
It is as though that were obtained that may not be.
ta:
உறாஅதோ ஊரறிந்த கெளவை அதனைப்
  பெறாஅது பெற்றன்ன நீர்த்து.

commentary: Rumour increases the violence of my passion; without it it would grow weak and waste away.
en:
The rumour rising makes my love to rise;
My love would lose its power and languish otherwise.
ta:
கவ்வையால் கவ்விது காமம் அதுவின்றேல்
  தவ்வென்னும் தன்மை இழந்து.

commentary: As drinking liquor is delightful (to one) whenever one is in mirth, so is lust delightful to me whenever it is the subject of rumour.
en:
The more man drinks, the more he ever drunk would be;
The more my love's revealed, the sweeter 'tis to me!
ta:
களித்தொறும் கள்ளுண்டல் வேட்டற்றால் காமம்
  வெளிப்படுந் தோறும் இனிது.

commentary: It was but a single day that I looked on (my lover); but the rumour thereof has spread like the seizure of the moon by the serpent.
en:
I saw him but one single day: rumour spreads soon
As darkness, when the dragon seizes on the moon.
ta:
கண்டது மன்னும் ஒருநாள் அலர்மன்னும்
  திங்களைப் பாம்புகொண் டற்று.

commentary: This malady (of lust) is manured by the talk of women and watered by the (harsh) words of my mother.
en:
My anguish grows apace: the town's report
Manures it; my mother's word doth water it.
ta:
ஊரவர் கெளவை எருவாக அன்னைசொல்
  நீராக நீளும்இந் நோய்.

commentary: To say that one could extinguish passion by rumour is like extinguishing fire with ghee.
en:
With butter-oil extinguish fire! 'Twill prove
Harder by scandal to extinguish love.
ta:
நெய்யால் எரிநுதுப்பேம் என்றற்றால் கெளவையால்
  காமம் நுதுப்பேம் எனல்.

commentary: When the departure of him who said "fear not" has put me to shame before others, why need I be ashamed of scandal.
en:
When he who said 'Fear not!' hath left me blamed,
While many shrink, can I from rumour hide ashamed?
ta:
அலர்நாண ஒல்வதோ அஞ்சலோம்பு என்றார்
  பலர்நாண நீத்தக் கடை.

commentary: The rumour I desire is raised by the town (itself); and my lover would if desired consent (to my following him).
en:
If we desire, who loves will grant what we require;
This town sends forth the rumour we desire!
ta:
தாம்வேண்டின் நல்குவர் காதலர் யாம்வேண்டும்
  கெளவை எடுக்கும்இவ் வூர்.

commentary: If it is not departure, tell me; but if it is your speedy return, tell it to those who would be alive then.
en:
If you will say, 'I leave thee not,' then tell me so;
Of quick return tell those that can survive this woe.
ta:
செல்லாமை உண்டேல் எனக்குரை மற்றுநின்
  வல்வரவு வாழ்வார்க் குரை.

commentary: His very look was once pleasing; but (now) even intercourse is painful through fear of separation.
en:
It once was perfect joy to look upon his face;
But now the fear of parting saddens each embrace.
ta:
இன்கண் உடைத்தவர் பார்வல் பிரிவஞ்சும்
  புன்கண் உடைத்தால் புணர்வு.

commentary: As even the lover who understands (everything) may at times depart, confidence is hardly possible.
en:
To trust henceforth is hard, if ever he depart,
E'en he, who knows his promise and my breaking heart.
ta:
அரிதரோ தேற்றம் அறிவுடையார் கண்ணும்
  பிரிவோ ரிடத்துண்மை யான்.

commentary: If he who bestowed his love and said "fear not" should depart, will it be the fault of those who believed in (his) assuring words ?
en:
If he depart, who fondly said, 'Fear not,' what blame's incurred
By those who trusted to his reassuring word?
ta:
அளித்தஞ்சல் என்றவர் நீப்பின் தெளித்தசொல்
  தேறியார்க்கு உண்டோ தவறு.

commentary: If you would save (my life), delay the departure of my destined (husband); for if he departs, intercourse will become impossible.
en:
If you would guard my life, from going him restrain
Who fills my life! If he depart, hardly we meet again.
ta:
ஓம்பின் அமைந்தார் பிரிவோம்பல் மற்றவர்
  நீங்கின் அரிதால் புணர்வு.

commentary: If he is so cruel as to mention his departure (to me), the hope that he would bestow (his love) must be given up.
en:
To cherish longing hope that he should ever gracious be,
Is hard, when he could stand, and of departure speak to me.
ta:
பிரிவுரைக்கும் வன்கண்ணர் ஆயின் அரிதவர்
  நல்குவர் என்னும் நசை.

commentary: Do not the rings that begin to slide down my fingers forebode the separation of my lord ?
en:
The bracelet slipping from my wrist announced before
Departure of the Prince that rules the ocean shore.
ta:
துறைவன் துறந்தமை தூற்றாகொல் முன்கை
  இறைஇறவா நின்ற வளை.

commentary: Painful is it to live in a friendless town; but far more painful is it to part from one's lover.
en:
'Tis sad to sojourn in the town where no kind kinsmen dwell;
'Tis sadder still to bid a friend beloved farewell.
ta:
இன்னாது இனன்இல்ஊர் வாழ்தல் அதனினும்
  இன்னாது இனியார்ப் பிரிவு.

commentary: Fire burns when touched; but, like the sickness of love, can it also burn when removed ?
en:
Fire burns the hands that touch; but smart of love
Will burn in hearts that far away remove.
ta:
தொடிற்சுடின் அல்லது காமநோய் போல
  விடிற்சுடல் ஆற்றுமோ தீ.

commentary: As if there were many indeed that can consent to the impossible, kill their pain, endure separation and yet continue to live afterwards.
en:
Sorrow's sadness meek sustaining, Driving sore distress away,
Separation uncomplaining Many bear the livelong day!
ta:
அரிதாற்றி அல்லல்நோய் நீக்கிப் பிரிவாற்றிப்
  பின்இருந்து வாழ்வார் பலர்.

commentary: I would hide this pain from others; but it (only) swells like a spring to those who drain it.
en:
I would my pain conceal, but see! it surging swells,
As streams to those that draw from ever-springing wells.
ta:
மறைப்பேன்மன் யானிஃதோ நோயை இறைப்பவர்க்கு
  ஊற்றுநீர் போல மிகும்.

commentary: I cannot conceal this pain, nor can I relate it without shame to him who has caused it.
en:
I cannot hide this pain of mine, yet shame restrains
When I would tell it out to him who caused my pains.
ta:
கரத்தலும் ஆற்றேன்இந் நோயைநோய் செய்தார்க்கு
  உரைத்தலும் நாணுத் தரும்.

commentary: (Both) lust and shame, with my soul for their shoulder pole balance themselves on a body that cannot bear them.
en:
My soul, like porter's pole, within my wearied frame,
Sustains a two-fold burthen poised, of love and shame.
ta:
காமமும் நாணும் உயிர்காவாத் தூங்கும்என்
  நோனா உடம்பின் அகத்து.

commentary: There is indeed a flood of lust; but there is no raft of safety to cross it with.
en:
A sea of love, 'tis true, I see stretched out before,
But not the trusty bark that wafts to yonder shore.
ta:
காமக் கடல்மன்னும் உண்டே அதுநீந்தும்
  ஏமப் புணைமன்னும் இல்.

commentary: He who can produce sorrow from friendship, what can he not bring forth out of enmity ?
en:
Who work us woe in friendship's trustful hour,
What will they prove when angry tempests lower?
ta:
துப்பின் எவனாவர் மன்கொல் துயர்வரவு
  நட்பினுள் ஆற்று பவர்.

commentary: The pleasure of lust is (as great as) the sea; but the pain of lust is far greater.
en:
A happy love 's sea of joy; but mightier sorrows roll
From unpropitious love athwart the troubled soul.
ta:
இன்பம் கடல்மற்றுக் காமம் அஃதடுங்கால்
  துன்பம் அதனிற் பெரிது.

commentary: I have swam across the terrible flood of lust, but have not seen its shore; even at midnight I am alone; still I live.
en:
I swim the cruel tide of love, and can no shore descry,
In watches of the night, too, 'mid the waters, only I!
ta:
காமக் கடும்புனல் நீந்திக் கரைகாணேன்
  யாமத்தும் யானே உளேன்.

commentary: The night which graciously lulls to sleep all living creatures, has me alone for her companion.
en:
All living souls in slumber soft she steeps;
But me alone kind night for her companing keeps!
ta:
மன்னுயிர் எல்லாம் துயிற்றி அளித்திரா
  என்னல்லது இல்லை துணை.

commentary: The long nights of these days are far more cruel than the heartless one who is torturing me.
en:
More cruel than the cruelty of him, the cruel one,
In these sad times are lengthening hours of night I watch alone.
ta:
கொடியார் கொடுமையின் தாம்கொடிய விந்நாள்
  நெடிய கழியும் இரா.

commentary: Could mine eyes travel like my thoughts to the abode (of my absent lord), they would not swim in this flood of tears.
en:
When eye of mine would as my soul go forth to him,
It knows not how through floods of its own tears to swim.
ta:
உள்ளம்போன்று உள்வழிச் செல்கிற்பின் வெள்ளநீர்
  நீந்தல மன்னோஎன் கண்.

commentary: As this incurable malady has been caused by my eyes which showed (him) to me, why should they now weep for (him).
en:
They showed me him, and then my endless pain
I saw: why then should weeping eyes complain?
ta:
கண்தாம் கலுழ்வ தெவன்கொலோ தண்டாநோய்
  தாம்காட்ட யாம்கண் டது.

commentary: The dyed eyes that (then) looked without foresight, why should they now endure sorrow, without feeling sharply (their own fault).
en:
How glancing eyes, that rash unweeting looked that day,
With sorrow measureless are wasting now away!
ta:
தெரிந்துணரா நோக்கிய உண்கண் பரிந்துணராப்
  பைதல் உழப்பது எவன்.

commentary: They themselves looked eagerly (on him) and now they weep. Is not this to be laughed at ?
en:
The eyes that threw such eager glances round erewhile
Are weeping now. Such folly surely claims a smile!
ta:
கதுமெனத் தாநோக்கித் தாமே கலுழும்
  இதுநகத் தக்க துடைத்து.

commentary: These painted eyes have caused me a lasting mortal disease; and now they can weep no more, the tears having dried up.
en:
Those eyes have wept till all the fount of tears is dry,
That brought upon me pain that knows no remedy.
ta:
பெயலாற்றா நீருலந்த உண்கண் உயலாற்றா
  உய்வில்நோய் என்கண் நிறுத்து.

commentary: Mine eyes have caused me a lust that is greater than the sea and (they themselves) endure the torture of sleeplessness.
en:
The eye that wrought me more than sea could hold of woes,
Is suffering pangs that banish all repose.
ta:
படலாற்றா பைதல் உழக்கும் கடலாற்றாக்
  காமநோய் செய்தஎன் கண்.

commentary: The eyes that have given me this disease have themselves been seized with this (suffering). Oh! I am much delighted.
en:
Oho! how sweet a thing to see! the eye
That wrought this pain, in the same gulf doth lie.
ta:
ஓஒ இனிதே எமக்கிந்நோய் செய்தகண்
  தாஅம் இதற்பட் டது.

commentary: The eyes that became tender and gazed intently on him, may they suffer so much as to dry up the fountain of their tears.
en:
Aching, aching, let those exhaust their stream,
That melting, melting, that day gazed on him.
ta:
உழந்துழந் துள்நீர் அறுக விழைந்திழைந்து
  வேண்டி அவர்க்கண்ட கண்.

commentary: He is indeed here who loved me with his lips but not with his heart but mine eyes suffer from not seeing him.
en:
Who loved me once, onloving now doth here remain;
Not seeing him, my eye no rest can gain.
ta:
பேணாது பெட்டார் உளர்மன்னோ மற்றவர்க்
  காணாது அமைவில கண்.

commentary: When he is away they do not sleep; when he is present they do not sleep; in either case, mine eyes endure unbearable agony.
en:
When he comes not, all slumber flies; no sleep when he is there;
Thus every way my eyes have troubles hard to bear.
ta:
வாராக்கால் துஞ்சா வரின்துஞ்சா ஆயிடை
  ஆரஞர் உற்றன கண்.

commentary: It is not difficult for the people of this place to understand the secret of those whose eyes, like mine, are as it were beaten drums.
en:
It is not hard for all the town the knowledge to obtain,
When eyes, as mine, like beaten tambours, make the mystery plain.
ta:
மறைபெறல் ஊரார்க்கு அரிதன்றால் எம்போல்
  அறைபறை கண்ணார் அகத்து.

commentary: I who (then) consented to the absence of my loving lord, to whom can I (now) relate the fact of my having turned sallow.
en:
I willed my lover absent should remain;
Of pining's sickly hue to whom shall I complain?
ta:
நயந்தவர்க்கு நல்காமை நேர்ந்தேன் பசந்தவென்
  பண்பியார்க்கு உரைக்கோ பிற.

commentary: Sallowness, as if proud of having been caused by him, would now ride on my person.
en:
'He gave': this sickly hue thus proudly speaks,
Then climbs, and all my frame its chariot makes.
ta:
அவர்தந்தார் என்னும் தகையால் இவர்தந்தென்
  மேனிமேல் ஊரும் பசப்பு.

commentary: He has taken (away) my beauty and modesty, and given me instead disease and sallowness.
en:
Of comeliness and shame he me bereft,
While pain and sickly hue, in recompense, he left.
ta:
சாயலும் நாணும் அவர்கொண்டார் கைம்மாறா
  நோயும் பசலையும் தந்து.

commentary: I think (of him); and what I speak about is but his excellence; still is there sallowness; and this is deceitful.
en:
I meditate his words, his worth is theme of all I say,
This sickly hue is false that would my trust betray.
ta:
உள்ளுவன் மன்யான் உரைப்பது அவர்திறமால்
  கள்ளம் பிறவோ பசப்பு.

commentary: Just as my lover departed then, did not sallowness spread here on my person ?
en:
My lover there went forth to roam;
This pallor of my frame usurps his place at home.
ta:
உவக்காண்எம் காதலர் செல்வார் இவக்காண்என்
  மேனி பசப்பூர் வது.

commentary: Just as darkness waits for the failing light; so does sallowness wait for the laxity of my husband's intercourse.
en:
As darkness waits till lamp expires, to fill the place,
This pallor waits till I enjoy no more my lord's embrace.
ta:
விளக்கற்றம் பார்க்கும் இருளேபோல் கொண்கன்
  முயக்கற்றம் பார்க்கும் பசப்பு.

commentary: I who was in close embrace just turned aside and the moment I did so, sallowness came on me like something to be seized on.
en:
I lay in his embrace, I turned unwittingly;
Forthwith this hue, as you might grasp it, came on me.
ta:
புல்லிக் கிடந்தேன் புடைபெயர்ந்தேன் அவ்வளவில்
  அள்ளிக்கொள் வற்றே பசப்பு.

commentary: Besides those who say "she has turned sallow" there are none who say "he has forsaken her".
en:
On me, because I pine, they cast a slur;
But no one says, 'He first deserted her.'
ta:
பசந்தாள் இவள்என்பது அல்லால் இவளைத்
  துறந்தார் அவர்என்பார் இல்.

commentary: If he is clear of guilt who has conciliated me (to his departure) let my body suffer its due and turn sallow.
en:
Well! let my frame, as now, be sicklied o'er with pain,
If he who won my heart's consent, in good estate remain!
ta:
பசக்கமன் பட்டாங்கென் மேனி நயப்பித்தார்
  நன்னிலையர் ஆவர் எனின்.

commentary: It would be good to be said of me that I have turned sallow, if friends do not reproach with unkindness him who pleased me (then).
en:
'Tis well, though men deride me for my sickly hue of pain;
If they from calling him unkind, who won my love, refrain.
ta:
பசப்பெனப் பேர்பெறுதல் நன்றே நயப்பித்தார்
  நல்காமை தூற்றார் எனின்.

commentary: The women who are beloved by those whom they love, have they have not got the stone-less fruit of sexual delight ?
en:
The bliss to be beloved by those they love who gains,
Of love the stoneless, luscious fruit obtains.
ta:
தாம்வீழ்வார் தம்வீழப் பெற்றவர் பெற்றாரே
  காமத்துக் காழில் கனி.

commentary: The bestowal of love by the beloved on those who love them is like the rain raining (at the proper season) on those who live by it.
en:
As heaven on living men showers blessings from above,
Is tender grace by lovers shown to those they love.
ta:
வாழ்வார்க்கு வானம் பயந்தற்றால் வீழ்வார்க்கு
  வீழ்வார் அளிக்கும் அளி.

commentary: The pride that says "we shall live" suits only those who are loved by their beloved (husbands).
en:
Who love and are beloved to them alone
Belongs the boast, 'We've made life's very joys our own.'
ta:
வீழுநர் வீழப் படுவார்க்கு அமையுமே
  வாழுநம் என்னும் செருக்கு.

commentary: Even those who are esteemed (by other women) are devoid of excellence, if they are not loved by their beloved.
en:
Those well-beloved will luckless prove,
Unless beloved by those they love.
ta:
வீழப் படுவார் கெழீஇயிலர் தாம்வீழ்வார்
  வீழப் படாஅர் எனின்.

commentary: He who is beloved by me, what will he do to me, if I am not beloved by him ?
en:
From him I love to me what gain can be,
Unless, as I love him, he loveth me?
ta:
நாம்காதல் கொண்டார் நமக்கெவன் செய்பவோ
  தாம்காதல் கொள்ளாக் கடை.

commentary: Lust, like the weight of the KAVADI, pains if it lies in one end only but pleases if it is in both.
en:
Love on one side is bad; like balanced load
By porter borne, love on both sides is good.
ta:
ஒருதலையான் இன்னாது காமம்காப் போல
  இருதலை யானும் இனிது.

commentary: Would not cupid who abides and contends in one party (only) witness the pain and sorrow (in that party)?
en:
While Kaman rushes straight at me alone,
Is all my pain and wasting grief unknown?
ta:
பருவரலும் பைதலும் காணான்கொல் காமன்
  ஒருவர்கண் நின்றொழுகு வான்.

commentary: There is no one in the world so hard-hearted as those who can live without receiving (even) a kind word from their beloved.
en:
Who hear from lover's lips no pleasant word from day to day,
Yet in the world live out their life,- no braver souls than they!
ta:
வீழ்வாரின் இன்சொல் பெறாஅது உலகத்து
  வாழ்வாரின் வன்கணார் இல்.

commentary: Though my beloved bestows no love on one, still are his words sweet to my ears.
en:
Though he my heart desires no grace accords to me,
Yet every accent of his voice is melody.
ta:
நசைஇயார் நல்கார் எனினும் அவர்மாட்டு
  இசையும் இனிய செவிக்கு.

commentary: Live, O my soul, would you who relate your great sorrow to strangers, try rather to fill up your own sea (of sorrow).
en:
Tell him thy pain that loves not thee?
Farewell, my soul, fill up the sea!
ta:
உறாஅர்க்கு உறுநோய் உரைப்பாய் கடலைச்
  செறாஅஅய் வாழிய நெஞ்சு.

commentary: Sexuality is sweeter than liquor, because when remembered, it creates a most rapturous delight.
en:
From thought of her unfailing gladness springs,
Sweeter than palm-rice wine the joy love brings.
ta:
உள்ளினும் தீராப் பெருமகிழ் செய்தலால்
  கள்ளினும் காமம் இனிது.

commentary: Even to think of one's beloved gives one no pain. Sexuality, in any degree, is always delightful.
en:
How great is love! Behold its sweetness past belief!
Think on the lover, and the spirit knows no grief.
ta:
எனைத்தொனறு ஏனிதேகாண் காமம்தாம் வீழ்வார்
  நினைப்ப வருவதொன்று ஏல்.

commentary: I feel as if I am going to sneeze but do not, and (therefore) my beloved is about to think (of me) but does not.
en:
A fit of sneezing threatened, but it passed away;
He seemed to think of me, but do his fancies stray?
ta:
நினைப்பவர் போன்று நினையார்கொல் தும்மல்
  சினைப்பது போன்று கெடும்.

commentary: He continues to abide in my soul, do I likewise abide in his ?
en:
Have I a place within his heart!
From mine, alas! he never doth depart.
ta:
யாமும் உளேங்கொல் அவர்நெஞ்சத்து எந்நெஞ்சத்து
  ஓஒ உளரே அவர்.

commentary: He who has imprisoned me in his soul, is he ashamed to enter incessantly into mine.
en:
Me from his heart he jealously excludes:
Hath he no shame who ceaseless on my heart intrudes?
ta:
தம்நெஞ்சத்து எம்மைக் கடிகொண்டார் நாணார்கொல்
  எம்நெஞ்சத்து ஓவா வரல்.

commentary: I live by remembering my (former) intercourse with him; if it were not so, how could I live ?
en:
How live I yet? I live to ponder o'er
The days of bliss with him that are no more.
ta:
மற்றியான் என்னுளேன் மன்னோ அவரொடியான்
  உற்றநாள் உள்ள உளேன்.

commentary: I have never forgotten (the pleasure); even to think of it burns my soul; could I live, if I should ever forget it ?
en:
If I remembered not what were I then? And yet,
The fiery smart of what my spirit knows not to forget!
ta:
மறப்பின் எவனாவன் மற்கொல் மறப்பறியேன்
  உள்ளினும் உள்ளம் சுடும்.

commentary: He will not be angry however much I may think of him; is it not so much the delight my beloved affords me ?
en:
My frequent thought no wrath excites. It is not so?
This honour doth my love on me bestow.
ta:
எனைத்து நினைப்பினும் காயார் அனைத்தன்றோ
  காதலர் செய்யும் சிறப்பு.

commentary: My precious life is wasting away by thinking too much on the cruelty of him who said we were not different.
en:
Dear life departs, when his ungracious deeds I ponder o'er,
Who said erewhile, 'We're one for evermore'.
ta:
விளியுமென் இன்னுயிர் வேறல்லம் என்பார்
  அளியின்மை ஆற்ற நினைந்து.

commentary: May you live, O Moon! Do not set, that I mine see him who has departed without quitting my soul.
en:
Set not; so may'st thou prosper, moon! that eyes may see
My love who went away, but ever bides with me.
ta:
விடாஅது சென்றாரைக் கண்ணினால் காணப்
  படாஅதி வாழி மதி.

commentary: Where with shall I feast the dream which has brought me my dear one's messenger ?
en:
It came and brought to me, that nightly vision rare,
A message from my love,- what feast shall I prepare?
ta:
காதலர் தூதொடு வந்த கனவினுக்கு
  யாதுசெய் வேன்கொல் விருந்து.

commentary: If my fish-like painted eyes should, at my begging, close in sleep, I could fully relate my sufferings to my lord.
en:
If my dark, carp-like eye will close in sleep, as I implore,
The tale of my long-suffering life I'll tell my loved one o'er.
ta:
கயலுண்கண் யானிரப்பத் துஞ்சிற் கலந்தார்க்கு
  உயலுண்மை சாற்றுவேன் மன்.

commentary: My life lasts because in my dream I behold him who does not favour me in my waking hours.
en:
Him, who in waking hour no kindness shows,
In dreams I see; and so my lifetime goes!
ta:
நனவினால் நல்கா தவரைக் கனவினால்
  காண்டலின் உண்டென் உயிர்.

commentary: There is pleasure in my dream, because in it I seek and obtain him who does not visit me in my wakefulness.
en:
Some pleasure I enjoy when him who loves not me
In waking hours, the vision searches out and makes me see.
ta:
கனவினான் உண்டாகும் காமம் நனவினான்
  நல்காரை நாடித் தரற்கு.

commentary: I saw him in my waking hours, and then it was pleasant; I see him just now in my dream, and it is (equally) pleasant.
en:
As what I then beheld in waking hour was sweet,
So pleasant dreams in hour of sleep my spirit greet.
ta:
நனவினால் கண்டதூஉம் ஆங்கே கனவுந்தான்
  கண்ட பொழுதே இனிது.

commentary: Were there no such thing as wakefulness, my beloved (who visited me) in my dream would not depart from me.
en:
And if there were no waking hour, my love
In dreams would never from my side remove.
ta:
நனவென ஒன்றில்லை ஆயின் கனவினால்
  காதலர் நீங்கலர் மன்.

commentary: The cruel one who would not favour me in my wakefulness, what right has he to torture me in my dreams?
en:
The cruel one, in waking hour, who all ungracious seems,
Why should he thus torment my soul in nightly dreams?
ta:
நனவினால் நல்காக் கொடியார் கனவனால்
  என்எம்மைப் பீழிப் பது.

commentary: When I am asleep he rests on my shoulders, (but) when I awake he hastens into my soul.
en:
And when I sleep he holds my form embraced;
And when I wake to fill my heart makes haste!
ta:
துஞ்சுங்கால் தோள்மேலர் ஆகி விழிக்குங்கால்
  நெஞ்சத்தர் ஆவர் விரைந்து.

commentary: They who have no dear ones to behold in their dreams blame him who visits me not in my waking hours.
en:
In dreams who ne'er their lover's form perceive,
For those in waking hours who show no love will grieve.
ta:
நனவினால் நல்காரை நோவர் கனவினால்
  காதலர்க் காணா தவர்.

commentary: The women of this place say he has forsaken me in my wakefulness. I think they have not seen him visit me in my dreams.
en:
They say, that he in waking hours has left me lone;
In dreams they surely see him not,- these people of the town;
ta:
நனவினால் நம்நீத்தார் என்பர் கனவினால்
  காணார்கொல் இவ்வூ ரவர்.

commentary: Live, O you evening are you (the former) evening? No, you are the season that slays (married) women.
en:
Thou art not evening, but a spear that doth devour
The souls of brides; farewell, thou evening hour!
ta:
மாலையோ அல்லை மணந்தார் உயிருண்ணும்
  வேலைநீ வாழி பொழுது.

commentary: A long life to you, O dark evening! You are sightless. Is your help-mate (also) as hard-hearted as mine.
en:
Thine eye is sad; Hail, doubtful hour of eventide!
Of cruel eye, as is my spouse, is too thy bride?
ta:
புன்கண்ணை வாழி மருள்மாலை எம்கேள்போல்
  வன்கண்ண தோநின் துணை.

commentary: The evening that (once) came in with trembling and dimness (now) brings me an aversion for life and increasing sorrow.
en:
With buds of chilly dew wan evening's shade enclose;
My anguish buds space and all my sorrow grows.
ta:
பனிஅரும்பிப் பைதல்கொள் மாலை துனிஅரும்பித்
  துன்பம் வளர வரும்.

commentary: In the absence of my lover, evening comes in like slayers on the field of slaughter.
en:
When absent is my love, the evening hour descends,
As when an alien host to field of battle wends.
ta:
காதலர் இல்வழி மாலை கொலைக்களத்து
  ஏதிலர் போல வரும்.

commentary: What good have I done to morning (and) what evil to evening?
en:
O morn, how have I won thy grace? thou bring'st relief
O eve, why art thou foe! thou dost renew my grief.
ta:
காலைக்குச் செய்தநன்று என்கொல் எவன்கொல்யான்
  மாலைக்குச் செய்த பகை.

commentary: Previous to my husband's departure, I know not the painful nature of evening.
en:
The pangs that evening brings I never knew,
Till he, my wedded spouse, from me withdrew.
ta:
மாலைநோய் செய்தல் மணந்தார் அகலாத
  காலை அறிந்த திலேன்.

commentary: This malady buds forth in the morning, expands all day long and blossoms in the evening.
en:
My grief at morn a bud, all day an opening flower,
Full-blown expands in evening hour.
ta:
காலை அரும்பிப் பகலெல்லாம் போதாகி
  மாலை மலரும்இந் நோய்.

commentary: The shepherd's flute now sounds as a fiery forerunner of night, and is become a weapon that slays (me).
en:
The shepherd's pipe is like a murderous weapon, to my ear,
For it proclaims the hour of ev'ning's fiery anguish near.
ta:
அழல்போலும் மாலைக்குத் தூதாகி ஆயன்
  குழல்போலும் கொல்லும் படை.

commentary: When night comes on confusing (everyone's) mind, the (whole) town will lose its sense and be plunged in sorrow.
en:
If evening's shades, that darken all my soul, extend;
From this afflicted town will would of grief ascend.
ta:
பதிமருண்டு பைதல் உழக்கும் மதிமருண்டு
  மாலை படர்தரும் போழ்து.

commentary: My (hitherto) unextinguished life is now lost in this bewildering night at the thought of him who has the nature of wealth.
en:
This darkening eve, my darkling soul must perish utterly;
Remembering him who seeks for wealth, but seeks not me.
ta:
பொருள்மாலை யாளரை உள்ளி மருள்மாலை
  மாயும்என் மாயா உயிர்.

commentary: While we endure the unbearable sorrow, your eyes weep for him who is gone afar, and shun (the sight of) fragrant flowers.
en:
Thine eyes grown dim are now ashamed the fragrant flow'rs to see,
Thinking on him, who wand'ring far, leaves us in misery.
ta:
சிறுமை நமக்கொழியச் சேட்சென்றார் உள்ளி
  நறுமலர் நாணின கண்.

commentary: The discoloured eyes that shed tears profusely seem to betray the unkindness of our beloved.
en:
The eye, with sorrow wan, all wet with dew of tears,
As witness of the lover's lack of love appears.
ta:
நயந்தவர் நல்காமை சொல்லுவ போலும்
  பசந்து பனிவாரும் கண்.

commentary: The shoulders that swelled on the day of our union (now) seem to announce our separation clearly (to the public).
en:
These withered arms, desertion's pangs abundantly display,
That swelled with joy on that glad nuptial day.
ta:
தணந்தமை சால அறிவிப்ப போலும்
  மணந்தநாள் வீங்கிய தோள்.

commentary: In the absence of your consort, your shoulders having lost their former beauty and fulness, your bracelets of pure gold have become loose.
en:
When lover went, then faded all their wonted charms,
And armlets' golden round slips off from these poor wasted arms.
ta:
பணைநீங்கிப் பைந்தொடி சோரும் துணைநீங்கித்
  தொல்கவின் வாடிய தோள்.

commentary: The (loosened) bracelets, and the shoulders from which the old beauty has faded, relate the cruelty of the pitiless one.
en:
These wasted arms, the bracelet with their wonted beauty gone,
The cruelty declare of that most cruel one.
ta:
கொடியார் கொடுமை உரைக்கும் தொடியொடு
  தொல்கவின் வாடிய தோள்.

commentary: I am greatly pained to hear you call him a cruel man, just because your shoulders are reduced and your bracelets loosened.
en:
I grieve, 'tis pain to me to hear him cruel chid,
Because the armlet from my wasted arm has slid.
ta:
தொடியொடு தோள்நெகிழ நோவல் அவரைக்
  கொடியர் எனக்கூறல் நொந்து.

commentary: Can you O my soul! gain glory by relating to the (so-called) cruel one the clamour of my fading shoulders?
en:
My heart! say ought of glory wilt thou gain,
If to that cruel one thou of thy wasted arms complain?
ta:
பாடுபெறுதியோ நெஞ்சே கொடியார்க்கென் வாடுதோட்
  பூசல் உரைத்து.

commentary: When I once loosened the arms that were in embrace, the forehead of the gold-braceleted women turned sallow.
en:
One day the fervent pressure of embracing arms I checked,
Grew wan the forehead of the maid with golden armlet decked.
ta:
முயங்கிய கைகளை ஊக்கப் பசந்தது
  பைந்தொடிப் பேதை நுதல்.

commentary: When but a breath of breeze penetrated our embrace, her large cool eyes became sallow.
en:
As we embraced a breath of wind found entrance there;
The maid's large liquid eyes were dimmed with care.
ta:
முயக்கிடைத் தண்வளி போழப் பசப்புற்ற
  பேதை பெருமழைக் கண்.

commentary: Was it at the sight of what the bright forehead had done that the sallowness of her eyes became sad?
en:
The dimness of her eye felt sorrow now,
Beholding what was done by that bright brow.
ta:
கண்ணின் பசப்போ பருவரல் எய்தின்றே
  ஒண்ணுதல் செய்தது கண்டு.

commentary: O my soul, will you not think and tell me some medicine be it what it may, that can cure this incurable malady?
en:
My heart, canst thou not thinking of some med'cine tell,
Not any one, to drive away this grief incurable?
ta:
நினைத்தொன்று சொல்லாயோ நெஞ்சே எனைத்தொன்றும்
  எவ்வநோய் தீர்க்கும் மருந்து.

commentary: May you live, O my soul! While he is without love, for you to suffer is (simple) folly.
en:
Since he loves not, thy smart
Is folly, fare thee well my heart!
ta:
காதல் அவரிலர் ஆகநீ நோவது
  பேதைமை வாழியென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: O my soul! why remain (here) and suffer thinking (of him)? There are no lewd thoughts (of you) in him who has caused you this disease of sorrow.
en:
What comes of sitting here in pining thought, O heart? He knows
No pitying thought, the cause of all these wasting woes.
ta:
இருந்துள்ளி என்பரிதல் நெஞ்சே பரிந்துள்ளல்
  பைதல்நோய் செய்தார்கண் இல்.

commentary: O my soul! take my eyes also with you, (if not), these would eat me up (in their desire) to see him.
en:
O rid me of these eyes, my heart; for they,
Longing to see him, wear my life away.
ta:
கண்ணும் கொளச்சேறி நெஞ்சே இவையென்னைத்
  தின்னும் அவர்க்காணல் உற்று.

commentary: O my soul! can he who loves not though he is beloved, be forsaken saying he hates me (now)?
en:
O heart, as a foe, can I abandon utterly
Him who, though I long for him, longs not for me?
ta:
செற்றார் எனக்கை விடல்உண்டோ நெஞ்சேயாம்
  உற்றால் உறாஅ தவர்.

commentary: O my soul! when you see the dear one who remove dislike by intercourse, you are displeased and continue to be so. Nay, your displeasure is (simply) false.
en:
My heart, false is the fire that burns; thou canst not wrath maintain,
If thou thy love behold, embracing, soothing all thy pain.
ta:
கலந்துணர்த்தும் காதலர்க் கண்டாற் புலந்துணராய்
  பொய்க்காய்வு காய்திஎன் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: O my good soul, give up either lust or honour, as for me I can endure neither.
en:
Or bid thy love, or bid thy shame depart;
For me, I cannot bear them both, my worthy heart!
ta:
காமம் விடுஒன்றோ நாண்விடு நன்னெஞ்சே
  யானோ பொறேன்இவ் விரண்டு.

commentary: You are a fool, O my soul! to go after my departed one, while you mourn that he is not kind enough to favour you.
en:
Thou art befooled, my heart, thou followest him who flees from thee;
And still thou yearning criest: 'He will nor pity show nor love to me.'
ta:
பரிந்தவர் நல்காரென்று ஏங்கிப் பிரிந்தவர்
  பின்செல்வாய் பேதைஎன் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: O my soul! to whom would you repair, while the dear one is within yourself?
en:
My heart! my lover lives within my mind;
Roaming, whom dost thou think to find?
ta:
உள்ளத்தார் காத லவரால் உள்ளிநீ
  யாருழைச் சேறியென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: If I retain in my heart him who has left me without befriending me, I shall lose even the (inward) beauty that remains.
en:
If I should keep in mind the man who utterly renounces me,
My soul must suffer further loss of dignity.
ta:
துன்னாத் துறந்தாரை நெஞ்சத்து உடையேமா
  இன்னும் இழத்தும் கவின்.

commentary: The axe of lust can break the door of chastity which is bolted with the bolt of modesty.
en:
Of womanly reserve love's axe breaks through the door,
Barred by the bolt of shame before.
ta:
காமக் கணிச்சி உடைக்கும் நிறையென்னும்
  நாணுத்தாழ் வீழ்த்த கதவு.

commentary: Even at midnight is my mind worried by lust, and this one thing, alas! is without mercy.
en:
What men call love is the one thing of merciless power;
It gives my soul no rest, e'en in the midnight hour.
ta:
காமம் எனவொன்றோ கண்ணின்றென் நெஞ்சத்தை
  யாமத்தும் ஆளும் தொழில்.

commentary: I would conceal my lust, but alas, it yields not to my will but breaks out like a sneeze.
en:
I would my love conceal, but like a sneeze
It shows itself, and gives no warning sign.
ta:
மறைப்பேன்மன் காமத்தை யானோ குறிப்பின்றித்
  தும்மல்போல் தோன்றி விடும்.

commentary: I say I would be firm, but alas, my malady breaks out from its concealment and appears in public.
en:
In womanly reserve I deemed myself beyond assail;
But love will come abroad, and casts away the veil.
ta:
நிறையுடையேன் என்பேன்மன் யானோஎன் காமம்
  மறையிறந்து மன்று படும்.

commentary: The dignity that would not go after an absent lover is not known to those who are sticken by love.
en:
The dignity that seeks not him who acts as foe,
Is the one thing that loving heart can never know.
ta:
செற்றார்பின் செல்லாப் பெருந்தகைமை காமநோய்
  உற்றார் அறிவதொன்று அன்று.

commentary: The sorrow I have endured by desiring to go after my absent lover, in what way is it excellent?
en:
My grief how full of grace, I pray you see!
It seeks to follow him that hateth me.
ta:
செற்றவர் பின்சேறல் வேண்டி அளித்தரோ
  எற்றென்னை உற்ற துயர்.

commentary: I know nothing like shame when my beloved does from love (just) what is desired (by me).
en:
No sense of shame my gladdened mind shall prove,
When he returns my longing heart to bless with love.
ta:
நாணென ஒன்றோ அறியலம் காமத்தால்
  பேணியார் பெட்ப செயின்.

commentary: Are not the enticing words of my trick-abounding roguish lover the weapon that breaks away my feminine firmness?
en:
The words of that deceiver, versed in every wily art,
Are instruments that break through every guard of woman's heart!
ta:
பன்மாயக் கள்வன் பணிமொழி அன்றோநம்
  பெண்மை உடைக்கும் படை.

commentary: I said I would feign dislike and so went (away); (but) I embraced him the moment I say my mind began to unite with him!
en:
'I 'll shun his greeting'; saying thus with pride away I went:
I held him in my arms, for straight I felt my heart relent.
ta:
புலப்பல் எனச்சென்றேன் புல்லினேன் நெஞ்சம்
  கலத்தல் உறுவது கண்டு.

commentary: Is it possible for those whose hearts melt like fat in the fire to say they can feign a strong dislike and remain so?
en:
'We 'll stand aloof and then embrace': is this for them to say,
Whose hearts are as the fat that in the blaze dissolves away?
ta:
நிணந்தீயில் இட்டன்ன நெஞ்சினார்க்கு உண்டோ
  புணர்ந்தூடி நிற்பேம் எனல்.

commentary: My finger has worn away by marking (on the wall) the days he has been absent while my eyes have lost their lustre and begin to fail.
en:
My eyes have lost their brightness, sight is dimmed; my fingers worn,
With nothing on the wall the days since I was left forlorn.
ta:
வாளற்றுப் புற்கென்ற கண்ணும் அவர்சென்ற
  நாளொற்றித் தேய்ந்த விரல்.

commentary: O you bright-jewelled maid, if I forget (him) today, my shoulders will lose their beauty even in the other life and make my bracelets loose.
en:
O thou with gleaming jewels decked, could I forget for this one day,
Henceforth these bracelets from my arms will slip, my beauty worn away.
ta:
இலங்கிழாய் இன்று மறப்பின்என் தோள்மேல்
  கலங்கழியும் காரிகை நீத்து.

commentary: I still live by longing for the arrival of him who has gone out of love for victory and with valour as his guide.
en:
On victory intent, His mind sole company he went;
And I yet life sustain! And long to see his face again!
ta:
உரன்நசைஇ உள்ளம் துணையாகச் சென்றார்
  வரல்நசைஇ இன்னும் உளேன்.

commentary: My heart is rid of its sorrow and swells with rapture to think of my absent lover returning with his love.
en:
'He comes again, who left my side, and I shall taste love's joy,'-
My heart with rapture swells, when thoughts like these my mind employ.
ta:
கூடிய காமம் பிரிந்தார் வரவுள்ளிக்
  கோடுகொ டேறுமென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: May I look on my lover till I am satisfied and thereafter will vanish the sallowness of my slender shoulders.
en:
O let me see my spouse again and sate these longing eyes!
That instant from my wasted frame all pallor flies.
ta:
காண்கமன் கொண்கனைக் கண்ணாரக் கண்டபின்
  நீங்கும்என் மென்தோள் பசப்பு.

commentary: May my husband return some day; and then will I enjoy (him) so as to destroy all this agonizing sorrow.
en:
O let my spouse but come again to me one day!
I'll drink that nectar: wasting grief shall flee away.
ta:
வருகமன் கொண்கன் ஒருநாள் பருகுவன்
  பைதல்நோய் எல்லாம் கெட.

commentary: On the return of him who is as dear as my eyes, am I displeased or am I to embrace (him); or am I to do both?
en:
Shall I draw back, or yield myself, or shall both mingled be,
When he returns, my spouse, dear as these eyes to me.
ta:
புலப்பேன்கொல் புல்லுவேன் கொல்லோ கலப்பேன்கொல்
  கண்அன்ன கேளிர் விரன்.

commentary: Let the king fight and gain (victories); (but) let me be united to my wife and feast the evening.
en:
O would my king would fight, o'ercome, devide the spoil;
At home, to-night, the banquet spread should crown the toil.
ta:
வினைகலந்து வென்றீக வேந்தன் மனைகலந்து
  மாலை அயர்கம் விருந்து.

commentary: To those who suffer waiting for the day of return of their distant lovers one day is as long as seven days.
en:
One day will seem like seven to those who watch and yearn
For that glad day when wanderers from afar return.
ta:
ஒருநாள் எழுநாள்போல் செல்லும்சேண் சென்றார்
  வருநாள்வைத்து ஏங்கு பவர்க்கு.

commentary: After (my wife) has died of a broken heart, what good will there be if she is to receive me, has received me, or has even embraced me?
en:
What's my return, the meeting hour, the wished-for greeting worth,
If she heart-broken lie, with all her life poured forth?
ta:
பெறின்என்னாம் பெற்றக்கால் என்னாம் உறினென்னாம்
  உள்ளம் உடைந்துக்கக் கால்.

commentary: Though you would conceal (your feelings), your painted eyes would not, for, transgressing (their bounds), they tell (me) something.
en:
Thou hid'st it, yet thine eye, disdaining all restraint,
Something, I know not, what, would utter of complaint.
ta:
கரப்பினுங் கையிகந் தொல்லாநின் உண்கண்
  உரைக்கல் உறுவதொன் றுண்டு.

commentary: Unusually great is the female simplicity of your maid whose beauty fills my eyes and whose shoulders resemble the bamboo.
en:
The simple one whose beauty fills mine eye, whose shoulders curve
Like bambu stem, hath all a woman's modest sweet reserve.
ta:
கண்ணிறைந்த காரிகைக் காம்பேர்தோட் பேதைக்குப்
  பெண்நிறைந்த நீர்மை பெரிது.

commentary: There is something that is implied in the beauty of this woman, like the thread that is visible in a garland of gems.
en:
As through the crystal beads is seen the thread on which they 're strung
So in her beauty gleams some thought cannot find a tongue.
ta:
மணியில் திகழ்தரு நூல்போல் மடந்தை
  அணியில் திகழ்வதொன்று உண்டு.

commentary: There is something in the unmatured smile of this maid like the fragrance that is contained in an unblossomed bud.
en:
As fragrance in the opening bud, some secret lies
Concealed in budding smile of this dear damsel's eyes.
ta:
முகைமொக்குள் உள்ளது நாற்றம்போல் பேதை
  நகைமொக்குள் உள்ளதொன் றுண்டு.

commentary: The well-meant departure of her whose bangles are tight-fitting contains a remedy that can cure my great sorrow.
en:
The secret wiles of her with thronging armlets decked,
Are medicines by which my raising grief is checked.
ta:
செறிதொடி செய்திறந்த கள்ளம் உறுதுயர்
  தீர்க்கும் மருந்தொன்று உடைத்து.

commentary: The embrace that fills me with comfort and gladness is capable of enduring (my former) sorrow and meditating on his want of love.
en:
While lovingly embracing me, his heart is only grieved:
It makes me think that I again shall live of love bereaved.
ta:
பெரிதாற்றிப் பெட்பக் கலத்தல் அரிதாற்றி
  அன்பின்மை சூழ்வ துடைத்து.

commentary: My bracelets have understood before me the (mental) separation of him who rules the cool seashore.
en:
My severance from the lord of this cool shore,
My very armlets told me long before.
ta:
தண்ணந் துறைவன் தணந்தமை நம்மினும்
  முன்னம் உணர்ந்த வளை.

commentary: It was but yesterday my lover departed (from me); and it is seven days since my complexion turned sallow.
en:
My loved one left me, was it yesterday?
Days seven my pallid body wastes away!
ta:
நெருநற்றுச் சென்றார்எம் காதலர் யாமும்
  எழுநாளேம் மேனி பசந்து.

commentary: She looked at her bracelets, her tender shoulders, and her feet; this was what she did there (significantly).
en:
She viewed her tender arms, she viewed the armlets from them slid;
She viewed her feet: all this the lady did.
ta:
தொடிநோக்கி மென்தோளும் நோக்கி அடிநோக்கி
  அஃதாண் டவள்செய் தது.

commentary: To express their love-sickness by their eyes and resort to begging bespeaks more than ordinary female excellence.
en:
To show by eye the pain of love, and for relief to pray,
Is womanhood's most womanly device, men say.
ta:
பெண்ணினால் பெண்மை உடைத்தென்ப கண்ணினால்
  காமநோய் சொல்லி இரவு.

commentary: To please by thought and cheer by sight is peculiar, not to liquor but lust.
en:
Gladness at the thought, rejoicing at the sight,
Not palm-tree wine, but love, yields such delight.
ta:
உள்ளக் களித்தலும் காண மகிழ்தலும்
  கள்ளுக்கில் காமத்திற் குண்டு.

commentary: If women have a lust that exceeds even the measure of the palmyra fruit, they will not desire (to feign) dislike even as much as the millet.
en:
When as palmyra tall, fulness of perfect love we gain,
Distrust can find no place small as the millet grain.
ta:
தினைத்துணையும் ஊடாமை வேண்டும் பனைத்துணையும்
  காமம் நிறைய வரின்.

commentary: Though my eyes disregard me and do what is pleasing to my husband, still will they not be satisfied unless they see him.
en:
Although his will his only law, he lightly value me,
My heart knows no repose unless my lord I see.
ta:
பேணாது பெட்பவே செய்யினும் கொண்கனைக்
  காணா தமையல கண்.

commentary: O my friend! I was prepared to feign displeasure but my mind forgetting it was ready to embrace him.
en:
My friend, I went prepared to show a cool disdain;
My heart, forgetting all, could not its love restrain.
ta:
ஊடற்கண் சென்றேன்மன் தோழி அதுமறந்து
  கூடற்கண் சென்றதுஎன் னெஞ்சு.

commentary: Like the eyes which see not the pencil that paints it, I cannot see my husband's fault (just) when I meet him.
en:
The eye sees not the rod that paints it; nor can I
See any fault, when I behold my husband nigh.
ta:
எழுதுங்கால் கோல்காணாக் கண்ணேபோல் கொண்கன்
  பழிகாணேன் கண்ட இடத்து.

commentary: When I see my husband, I do not see any faults; but when I do not see him, I do not see anything but faults.
en:
When him I see, to all his faults I 'm blind;
But when I see him not, nothing but faults I find.
ta:
காணுங்கால் காணேன் தவறாய காணாக்கால்
  காணேன் தவறல் லவை.

commentary: Like those who leap into a stream which they know will carry them off, why should a wife feign dislike which she knows cannot hold out long?
en:
As those of rescue sure, who plunge into the stream,
So did I anger feign, though it must falsehood seem?
ta:
உய்த்தல் அறிந்து புனல்பாய் பவரேபோல்
  பொய்த்தல் அறிந்தென் புலந்து.

commentary: O you rogue! your breast is to me what liquor is to those who rejoice in it, though it only gives them an unpleasant disgrace.
en:
Though shameful ill it works, dear is the palm-tree wine
To drunkards; traitor, so to me that breast of thine!
ta:
இளித்தக்க இன்னா செயினும் களித்தார்க்குக்
  கள்ளற்றே கள்வநின் மார்பு.

commentary: Sexual delight is more delicate than a flower, and few are those who understand its real nature.
en:
Love is tender as an opening flower. In season due
To gain its perfect bliss is rapture known to few.
ta:
மலரினும் மெல்லிது காமம் சிலர்அதன்
  செவ்வி தலைப்படு வார்.

commentary: She once feigned dislike in her eyes, but the warmth of her embrace exceeded my own.
en:
Her eye, as I drew nigh one day, with anger shone:
By love o'erpowered, her tenderness surpassed my own.
ta:
கண்ணின் துனித்தே கலங்கினாள் புல்லுதல்
  என்னினும் தான்விதுப் புற்று.

commentary: O my soul! although you have seen how his soul stands by him, how is it you do not stand by me?
en:
You see his heart is his alone
O heart, why not be all my own?
ta:
அவர்நெஞ்சு அவர்க்காதல் கண்டும் எவன்நெஞ்சே
  நீஎமக்கு ஆகா தது.

commentary: O my soul! although you have known him who does not love me, still do you go to him, saying "he will not be displeased."
en:
'Tis plain, my heart, that he 's estranged from thee;
Why go to him as though he were not enemy?
ta:
உறாஅ தவர்க்கண்ட கண்ணும் அவரைச்
  செறாஅரெனச் சேறியென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: O my soul! do you follow him at pleasure under the belief that the ruined have no friends?
en:
'The ruined have no friends, 'they say; and so, my heart,
To follow him, at thy desire, from me thou dost depart.
ta:
கெட்டார்க்கு நட்டார்இல் என்பதோ நெஞ்சேநீ
  பெட்டாங்கு அவர்பின் செலல்.

commentary: O my soul! you would not first seem sulky and then enjoy (him); who then would in future consult you about such things?
en:
'See, thou first show offended pride, and then submit,' I bade;
Henceforth such council who will share with thee my heart?
ta:
இனிஅன்ன நின்னொடு சூழ்வார்யார் நெஞ்சே
  துனிசெய்து துவ்வாய்காண் மற்று.

commentary: My soul fears when it is without him; it also fears when it is with him; it is subject to incessant sorrow.
en:
I fear I shall not gain, I fear to lose him when I gain;
And thus my heart endures unceasing pain.
ta:
பெறாஅமை அஞ்சும் பெறின்பிரிவு அஞ்சும்
  அறாஅ இடும்பைத்தென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: My mind has been (here) in order to eat me up (as it were) whenever I think of him in my solitude.
en:
My heart consumes me when I ponder lone,
And all my lover's cruelty bemoan.
ta:
தனியே இருந்து நினைத்தக்கால் என்னைத்
  தினிய இருந்ததென் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: I have even forgotten my modesty, having been caught in my foolish mind which is not dignified enough to forget him.
en:
Fall'n 'neath the sway of this ignoble foolish heart,
Which will not him forget, I have forgotten shame.
ta:
நாணும் மறந்தேன் அவர்மறக் கல்லாஎன்
  மாணா மடநெஞ்சிற் பட்டு.

commentary: My soul which clings to life thinks only of his (own) gain in the belief that it would be disgraceful for it to despise him.
en:
If I contemn him, then disgrace awaits me evermore;
My soul that seeks to live his virtues numbers o'er.
ta:
எள்ளின் இளிவாம்என்று எண்ணி அவர்திறம்
  உள்ளும் உயிர்க்காதல் நெஞ்சு.

commentary: Who would help me out of one's distress, when one's own soul refuses help to one?
en:
And who will aid me in my hour of grief,
If my own heart comes not to my relief?
ta:
துன்பத்திற்கு யாரே துணையாவார் தாமுடைய
  நெஞ்சந் துணையல் வழி.

commentary: It is hardly possible for strangers to behave like relations, when one's own soul acts like a stranger.
en:
A trifle is unfriendliness by aliens shown,
When our own heart itself is not our own!
ta:
தஞ்சம் தமரல்லர் ஏதிலார் தாமுடைய
  நெஞ்சம் தமரல் வழி.

commentary: Let us witness awhile his keen suffering; just feign dislike and embrace him not.
en:
Be still reserved, decline his profferred love;
